terms.definition: Basic self-care tasks such as dressing, bathing, toileting, feeding and grooming required for independent living.
terms.exampleSentence: The OT assessed the patient's activities of daily living before discharge planning.
terms.contextNotes: Often abbreviated as ADLs in clinical notes.

terms.definition: Complex skills supporting community living such as cooking, shopping, managing finances, medication and transport.
terms.exampleSentence: She required prompting for instrumental activities of daily living, particularly meal preparation.
terms.contextNotes: Higher-order than basic ADLs; commonly abbreviated IADL.

terms.definition: An 18-item ordinal scale rating motor and cognitive function during rehabilitation, scored from total assistance to independence.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient's Functional Independence Measure score improved from 54 to 92 over six weeks.
terms.contextNotes: 18 items; scored 1 (total assist) to 7 (independent).

terms.definition: A 10-item ordinal scale measuring performance in basic activities of daily living, widely used in stroke rehabilitation.
terms.exampleSentence: Her Barthel Index on admission was 35, indicating severe dependence in self-care.
terms.contextNotes: Scored out of 100; lower scores indicate greater dependence.

terms.definition: A semi-structured client-centred interview tool identifying occupational performance issues and rating performance and satisfaction.
terms.exampleSentence: The Canadian Occupational Performance Measure highlighted dressing and meal preparation as priority goals.
terms.contextNotes: Performance and satisfaction each scored 1-10.

terms.term: Assessment of Motor and Process Skills
terms.definition: An observational standardised assessment evaluating quality of motor and process skills during familiar ADL tasks.
terms.exampleSentence: The Assessment of Motor and Process Skills revealed reduced effort and efficiency during meal preparation.
terms.contextNotes: Requires calibrated rater training.
commonMistakes: Assessment of Motor and Processing Skills
Assesment of Motor and Process Skills
similarSounding: Assessment of Motor and Practice Skills
Assessment of Movement and Process Skills

terms.definition: A brief 30-point screening tool for mild cognitive impairment, assessing attention, memory, executive function and language.
terms.exampleSentence: His Montreal Cognitive Assessment score of 22 suggested mild cognitive impairment.
terms.contextNotes: Cut-off typically 26/30 for normal cognition.

terms.definition: A 30-point bedside cognitive screening tool covering orientation, registration, attention, recall and language.
terms.exampleSentence: The Mini-Mental State Examination score was 18, indicating moderate cognitive impairment.
terms.contextNotes: Less sensitive to mild impairment than MoCA.

terms.definition: A framework rating functional cognition on a six-level scale, guiding task adaptation and supervision needs.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient functioned at Allen Cognitive Level 4.2, requiring cued supervision for new tasks.
terms.contextNotes: Often assessed via the leather-lacing ACL screen.

terms.definition: A standardised questionnaire mapping a person's sensory processing patterns across modalities and behavioural responses.
terms.exampleSentence: The sensory profile indicated low registration and sensory avoiding behaviours.
terms.contextNotes: Identifies four quadrants: seeking, avoiding, sensitivity, registration.

terms.definition: A standardised or observational evaluation of legibility, speed, grip and motor planning in writing tasks.
terms.exampleSentence: The paediatric handwriting assessment revealed an immature pencil grip and reduced legibility.
terms.contextNotes: Common tools include DASH and Minnesota Handwriting Assessment.

terms.definition: Evaluation determining the need, type and fit of a custom or prefabricated orthosis to support, immobilise or correct a limb.
terms.exampleSentence: Following the splint assessment, a resting hand splint was fabricated for night-time use.
terms.contextNotes: Includes skin checks and wear schedule education.

terms.definition: An on-site evaluation of the home environment to identify access, safety and equipment needs prior to discharge.
terms.exampleSentence: The home assessment recommended a stairlift and grab rails in the bathroom.
terms.contextNotes: Often joint visit with physiotherapist or social worker.

terms.definition: A functional evaluation of safety, sequencing and mobility during meal preparation tasks in a kitchen environment.
terms.exampleSentence: The kitchen assessment showed safe use of the kettle but difficulty sequencing a hot meal.
terms.contextNotes: Often graded from cold drink to full hot meal.

terms.definition: An evaluation of a patient's ability to move safely between surfaces such as bed, chair, toilet and car.
terms.exampleSentence: The transfer assessment determined a banana board was required for bed-to-chair transfers.
terms.contextNotes: Documents level of assistance and equipment used.

terms.definition: Evaluation of seating, postural support and mobility needs to prescribe an appropriate manual or powered wheelchair.
terms.exampleSentence: Following the wheelchair assessment, a tilt-in-space wheelchair was recommended for postural support.
terms.contextNotes: Includes pressure mapping and postural evaluation.

terms.definition: Strategies and equipment used to prevent or manage pressure ulcers in immobile or high-risk patients.
terms.exampleSentence: The OT recommended a high-risk pressure care cushion and two-hourly repositioning.
terms.contextNotes: Risk often stratified using Waterlow or Braden scales.

terms.definition: Education and pacing strategies enabling people with chronic conditions to balance activity, rest and energy expenditure.
terms.exampleSentence: Fatigue management strategies including the 4 Ps were introduced for the patient with multiple sclerosis.
terms.contextNotes: Often summarised as the 4 Ps: prioritise, plan, pace, position.

terms.definition: Evaluation of attention, memory, executive function, perception and praxis to inform rehabilitation goals.
terms.exampleSentence: The cognitive assessment revealed reduced working memory and impaired executive planning.
terms.contextNotes: May combine standardised tools with functional observation.

terms.definition: Evaluation of visual, spatial, body scheme and praxis perception, often following stroke or brain injury.
terms.exampleSentence: The perceptual assessment identified left-sided unilateral neglect affecting self-care.
terms.contextNotes: Common tools include the OT-APST and Rivermead Perceptual Assessment Battery.

terms.definition: Examination of range, strength, tone, sensation and function of the shoulder, elbow, wrist and hand.
terms.exampleSentence: The upper limb assessment showed grade 2 spasticity and reduced active wrist extension.
terms.contextNotes: Often combined with ARAT or Fugl-Meyer measures.

terms.definition: Standardised evaluation of grip, pinch, dexterity and coordination informing therapy and orthosis prescription.
terms.exampleSentence: The hand function assessment recorded a reduced lateral pinch of 3 kg on the dominant side.
terms.contextNotes: Common tools: Jamar dynamometer, Purdue Pegboard, Nine-Hole Peg Test.

terms.definition: Structured evaluation of intrinsic and environmental factors contributing to a person's risk of falling.
terms.exampleSentence: The falls risk assessment identified polypharmacy and reduced lower-limb strength as key risks.
terms.contextNotes: NICE recommends multifactorial assessment after a fall in older adults.

terms.definition: Techniques and equipment used to move people or loads safely, minimising musculoskeletal injury risk to staff.
terms.exampleSentence: Manual handling training was completed before recommending hoist transfers.
terms.contextNotes: Governed in the UK by the Manual Handling Operations Regulations 1992.

terms.definition: The ability to carry out chosen activities of self-care, productivity and leisure in expected environments.
terms.exampleSentence: Therapy aimed to restore occupational performance in self-care and meaningful leisure roles.
terms.contextNotes: Central concept in the Canadian Model of Occupational Performance.

terms.definition: Breaking an activity into component steps to identify performance demands and grading opportunities.
terms.exampleSentence: Task analysis of meal preparation revealed difficulty with sequencing and bilateral coordination.
terms.contextNotes: Underpins activity grading and adaptation.

terms.definition: Adjusting the demand of an activity by altering complexity, duration or support to match the client's ability.
terms.exampleSentence: The dressing task was graded by allowing seated practice before standing.
terms.contextNotes: Distinct from adaptation, which changes the task or environment.

terms.definition: Fabrication or fitting of an orthosis to support, immobilise, stretch or correct soft tissue and joint position.
terms.exampleSentence: Splinting in extension was indicated to prevent contracture of the metacarpophalangeal joints.
terms.contextNotes: Often uses low-temperature thermoplastic in hand therapy.

terms.definition: A coordinated process preparing the patient and environment for safe transition from hospital to home or care setting.
terms.exampleSentence: Discharge planning included home assessment, equipment provision and family training.
terms.contextNotes: Often led by the OT for patients with functional needs.

terms.definition: Specialised aids that enable independence in daily activities, such as long-handled reachers or button hooks.
terms.exampleSentence: Adaptive equipment including a sock aid and long-handled sponge was issued at discharge.
terms.contextNotes: Often trialled before issue to confirm safe use.
